summary: My parents were both born in Bhutan, where many people of Nepalese origin, called the Lhotshampas, lived. During the 1990s, the Bhutanese government forced the Lhotshampas to leave by imprisoning, threatening, and torturing them, including my grandfather. We had houses and land for agriculture in Bhutan, but we were stripped of citizenship and forced to flee.

After being forced out of Bhutan, my family and many other Lhotshampas fled to Nepal, where we lived like homeless people along the Mai River. The Nepalese government allowed refugees to settle in places like Damak, and my family moved to Beldangi refugee camp. Life in Nepal was hard; we faced discrimination, lacked citizenship, and lived in bamboo houses relying on UN aid.

My parents' families, like many other Lhotshampas fleeing Bhutan, lived along the shore of the Mai River in Nepal after we were forced to leave Bhutan in the 1990s. It was a difficult time as we had left almost everything behind and lived without proper homes or citizenship.

The Nepalese government offered the Lhotshampas a place to live in Damak as refugees, and because of this, my parents' families moved to Beldangi 2 camp near Damak. This was where my parents got married and had my siblings and me. Damak became an important place in our refugee journey during the 1990s and 2000s.

My family lived in Beldangi refugee camp after moving from the Mai River area in Nepal. Life in the camp was very hard; we lived in bamboo houses with no money and depended on the United Nations for aid. It was also a place where my parents started their family through an arranged marriage, but we faced discrimination and had no citizenship.

While living in Beldangi refugee camp in Nepal, we relied heavily on the United Nations for basic necessities because we had no money or resources. The UN provided us with what little we had to survive during those difficult years as refugees. Their support was crucial for many families like mine.

In 2010, my family applied to come to the United States through the International Organization for Migration process, and after completing it, we arrived in 2011. The IOM helped facilitate our migration from Nepal to the US, which was a major turning point in our lives. Although I was sad to leave my friends and family behind, it opened new opportunities for us.

My family and I came to the United States in 2011 after living as refugees in Nepal for many years. Life here was challenging at first because we didn't know English and struggled to adjust, but over time, through hard work, especially by my mother, we improved our situation. After nine years, we were able to buy our own house, which was a huge achievement for us.

When we arrived in the United States, we settled in Minnesota, where life was tough at the beginning. We stayed with relatives before finding our own apartment, which was moldy and in an unsafe neighborhood near Rice Street. Despite these hardships, Minnesota became home, and my mother found work and eventually helped us become citizens here.

My mother found her first job in Minnesota working in the kitchen area at Sun Foods. This was after my father became ill and couldn't work, so she had to support the family despite not knowing English or having formal education. Her hard work at Sun Foods was the beginning of her journey to better employment and stability for us.

We lived near Rice Street in Minnesota in a moldy, dirty apartment in an unsafe area from 2011 until 2020. My mother worked at a thrift store near Rice Street after Sun Foods, earning low wages but persevering through the difficulties. Eventually, we moved away from Rice Street to a safer neighborhood, which greatly improved our quality of life.

After years of hard work, my mother was able to get a better-paying job at Andersen Corporation, which helped improve our family's financial situation. This job was a big step up from her previous work and showed how far we had come since arriving in the United States. It gave us more stability and hope for the future.

We lived in Saint Paul, Minnesota, near Rice Street when we first arrived, but the area was unsafe and the apartment conditions were poor. After many years, we moved away from Saint Paul to a safer place, which made us feel much more secure. Saint Paul was where we struggled and worked hard to build a new life in the United States.
